channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: U.S. Plan Is Said to Pull a Third of Fighter Jets It Provides NATO for Europe
item.description: The plan, outlined by officials and in a written document, provides rare clarity about the extent to which the Trump administration intends to reduce its commitment to NATO.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Louiza Vradi/Reuters
item.media:description: An American F-16 jet at Andravida air base, Greece, last year.

item.title: What the SpaceX I.P.O. Means for You, and a Giant Act of Vandalism in D.C.
item.description: Plus, the Friday news quiz.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart and Maureen Farrell
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: SpaceX, Elon Musk’s rocket company, will soon end up in index funds after rule changes by Nasdaq and other index providers.

item.title: How SpaceX’s IPO Compares With Saudi Aramco, Uber and Others
item.description: The previous record was set over six years ago. But SpaceX, whose stock begins trading on Friday, is on track to raise tens of billions of dollars more.
item.dc:creator: Jacqueline Gu
item.media:credit: Jacqueline Gu

item.title: SpaceX IPO: How Our Reporters Assess the Sky-High Valuation and Potential Economic Impact
item.description: Two Times tech reporters and their editor talk about assessing the sky-high valuation of the rocket and satellite maker, the potential economic impact of its public debut and the role of the world’s richest man in it all.
item.dc:creator: Megan DiTrolio
item.media:credit: Eric Gay/Associated Press
item.media:description: Elon Musk’s rocket maker SpaceX set a price of $135 a share, which would value it at $1.77 trillion.

item.title: A Dangerous Limbo Leaves Iran, and the World, Between Peace and War
item.description: Since announcing a nominal cease-fire two months ago, Iran, Israel and the U.S. have remained locked in low-intensity violence that has become a new normal.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: In Tehran on Monday, a billboard featuring the Iran theocracy’s first two supreme leaders loomed over passers-by.

item.title: The World Is Draining Oil Reserves, Raising Pressure for a Peace Deal
item.description: The amount of oil and fuel stored by businesses and governments has fallen sharply since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Lazaro Gamio
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Risk Strait of Hormuz or Wait? Shipping Companies Face a Costly Dilemma.
item.description: With more than 500 ships still stranded in the Persian Gulf, pressure on the shipowners and sailors is growing by the day.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Supplies of fresh food and water have been running low for the roughly 11,000 sailors stuck on hundreds of ships anchored in the Persian Gulf.

item.title: Kennedy Center Appeals Order to Remove Trump’s Name
item.description: One day before a deadline to take the president’s name off its facade, the arts institution appealed a federal judge’s ruling that also temporarily blocked it from closing.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Kevin Lamarque/Reuters
item.media:description: Judge Christopher R. Cooper ruled that only Congress had the power to alter the name of the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts, which was dedicated to the president in a 1964 law.

item.title: Some States Opt Out of Trump’s ‘Great American State Fair’
item.description: At least five have declined to participate, the latest sign that the national 250th birthday celebration has become a fragmented and partisan affair as the president seeks his imprint.
item.dc:creator: Eileen Sullivan
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction on the National Mall in preparation for the Great American State Fair.

item.title: Trump Suspends Funding for Los Angeles Homeless Agency
item.description: The Trump administration cited misspending among the reasons for blocking funds to the Los Angeles Homeless Services Authority. Mayor Karen Bass warned that “people will lose their lives.”
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Scott Turner, the housing secretary, said his agency would not fund what he called the “corrupt failure” of Los Angeles’s homelessness efforts.

item.title: Inside Cesar Chavez’s Labor Union, Women Faced a Culture of Sexual Abuse
item.description: Many of the women who helped lead the fight for Latino civil rights say they were quietly waging their own battles with harassment, misogyny and sexual assault inside the powerful United Farm Workers union.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Hurtes and Manny Fernandez
item.media:credit: Cayce Clifford for The New York Times

item.title: David Hockney, Who Restored the Human Form to Art, Dies at 88
item.description: His colorful figurative paintings were both conservative and iconoclastic, defying the dominant abstract schools of the mid-20th century.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter

item.title: What I Learned From David Hockney
item.description: The curator Norman Rosenthal knew the artist for over 60 years and still discovered something new when they collaborated on a final blockbuster show.
item.dc:creator: Norman Rosenthal
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Journalists looking at David Hockney’s work before the opening of the exhibition “David Hockney 25” at Fondation Louis Vuitton in Paris in April 2025.

item.title: With iPhones and Faxes, David Hockney Embraced Tech
item.description: Polaroids and photocopiers also gave the artist possibilities for creating in forms vastly different from his paintings.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: David Hockney; Photo credit: Richard Schmidt
item.media:description: “Mulholland Drive, June 1986,” a homemade print from a photocopier.

item.title: After Senate Loss, Cornyn Predicts ‘Miserable’ Final Two Years for Trump
item.description: In his first extensive interview since his defeat by a Trump-backed challenger, the Texas Republican said the Senate was in for a “bumpy ride” as he and others flex new political freedom.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: “There’s never going to be good enough for him, other than 100 percent, you know, slavish adherence to whatever he wants,” Senator John Cornyn said of President Trump. “But obviously that’s not what the senator’s role is supposed to be.”

item.title: Frisco Mayor’s Race Tests Anti-Muslim Politics in Texas
item.description: A runoff in the Dallas suburb of Frisco is testing whether anti-Muslim rhetoric, prominent in G.O.P. primaries this year, can win over a broader set of voters.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Campaign signs in support of candidate Rod Vilhauer in Frisco, Texas.

item.title: Excitement and Frustration Mix as the World Cup Comes to America
item.description: Astronomical ticket prices, soaring security costs and concern over traffic and transit snarls is mixed with pride in host cities and excitement over the U.S. team.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The United States plays Paraguay on Friday in the first U.S.-hosted game of the World Cup, at SoFi Stadium near Los Angeles. FIFA is requiring stadiums to hide the logos of their corporate sponsors during the tournament.

item.title: AIPAC Wants Democrats to Back Israel. Instead, They’re Turning on AIPAC.
item.description: Once the guardian of the bipartisan pro-Israel consensus, it is now a polarizing force in the party.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Mahler

item.title: Major League Baseball Will Question the Dodgers’s Doctor About Banned Drugs
item.description: The inquiry comes after a report that Dr. Neal ElAttrache, the physician for the team, supported the U.F.C. star Conor McGregor in using performance-enhancing drugs while recovering from an injury.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:credit: The Yomiuri Shimbun, via Associated Press Images
item.media:description: Dr. Neal ElAttrache with Shohei Ohtani of the Los Angeles Dodgers during a game in Tokyo in 2025.

item.title: Sheriffs in Maryland Challenge State Limits on Cooperation With ICE
item.description: A lawsuit by a group of 17 county law enforcement officers is another front in the Trump-era fight over local police’s role in immigration enforcement.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: Michelle Gustafson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Maryland’s Community Trust Act, among other measures, bars local officials from asking people about their immigration status.

item.title: Bring the Kids and Grab Some Barbecue: A Day at the Ukrainian Drone Races
item.description: Competitions that give soldiers a brief break from the front have a festival-like atmosphere, a mix of potentially deadly weapons and frolicking children.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: An audience watched the Wild Drones military drone-flying competition in Truskavets, Ukraine, last month.

item.title: Knicks in Five? Please Don’t Tempt the Jinx Gods.
item.description: Some Knicks fans have predicted series sweeps. Others, conditioned by decades of failure, pay more heed to jinxes and superstitious rituals.
item.dc:creator: Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Knicks’ championship run has revealed a fundamental difference among some fans, with some unafraid of making boastful predictions and others recoiling at the jinx factor of doing so.

item.title: Brawls, Biting and Bliss in a City Craving a Knicks Championship
item.description: Decades of bottled-up hopes and frustrations have created a frenzy around the New York Knicks that has brought out the worst in some fans.
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer and Maia Coleman
item.media:credit: José A. Alvarado Jr. for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police officers stood guard as Game 4 of the N.B.A. finals drew a massive number of fans to Midtown Manhattan.

item.title: Steven Spielberg on How to Build Suspense in ‘Disclosure Day’
item.description: The director narrates a scene from his movie that is a continuation, of sorts, of a sequence he filmed for his feature-length debut, “Duel,” in 1971.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy

item.title: ‘Disclosure Day’ Review: Spielberg Plays His Greatest Cosmic Hits
item.description: In a fast-paced sci-fi fantasy, infused with epic intentions and starring Emily Blunt, Josh O’Connor and Colman Domingo, the filmmaker brings the rest of us home.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: SpaceX’s I.P.O. Will Help Fulfill Elon Musk’s Political Vision of America
item.description: The SpaceX I.P.O. will enable its founder to build — and scale — his peculiar vision of society.
item.dc:creator: Amy Gamerman and Tam Stockton

item.title: Some Leftists Are Boycotting This Film. Everyone Should Watch It.
item.description: The inanity of the leftists who’d censor such a film shouldn’t distract us from the right-wing nightmare it reveals.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times

item.title: A Trillionaire?
item.description: SpaceX is expected to make Elon Musk even richer when its stock begins trading today.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton

item.title: Late Night Prepares for Trump’s U.F.C. Birthday Fight at the White House
item.description: Jimmy Fallon called the president “the only 80-year-old yelling, ‘Get on my lawn!’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Anti-Immigrant Riots after Belfast Stabbing Leave City on Edge
item.description: In two nights of violence in Northern Ireland after a brutal stabbing, people were targeted because of their skin color, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Peter Morrison/Associated Press
item.media:description: Attempting to clear protesters in Belfast, Northern Ireland, on Wednesday evening.
